levels.label: Pitch Curious
levels.copy: Core investor-pitch concepts are still unreliable. Use the answer review as a learning path before sending a deck into high-stakes meetings.

levels.label: Deck-Aware
levels.copy: You recognise some pitch vocabulary, but scenario application is inconsistent. Focus on the logic behind problem, evidence, ask, and Q&A.

levels.label: Pitch-Ready
levels.copy: You can handle the standard pitch sequence. The next edge is making every claim specific, evidenced, and connected to investor decision risk.

levels.label: Investor-Ready
levels.copy: Your pitch logic is strong. Stress-test the weakest dimension with a critical reviewer before relying on the deck in live fundraising.

levels.label: Narrative Operator
levels.copy: You understand the pitch as an investor decision process, not a slide performance. Keep calibrating the story against new evidence and harder questions.

recommendations.pitch-structure: Rewrite the deck as a ten-sentence narrative before editing slides.
recommendations.problem-and-customer: Narrow the customer segment, trigger, buyer, painful alternative, and why-now evidence.
recommendations.traction-and-ask: Connect traction, unit economics, round size, use of funds, and next financing milestone on one page.
recommendations.delivery-and-follow-up: Run a mock investor Q&A and prepare a follow-up note that answers the five hardest objections.

defaultSecondAction: Retake the challenge after revising the deck and practising Q&A with someone willing to challenge the story.
reflectionQuestion: Which slide would fail first if an investor asked for source evidence?
methodologyNote: The current version uses four dimensions with two scenario questions each. The best answer receives 3 points; plausible but incomplete answers may receive 1 point. Dimension scores are normalised to 0-100 and combined through the standard balanced index. This is an educational quiz, not investment advice or fundraising certification.
shareNote: Share cards reveal only the level and strongest area. They do not publish individual answers, deck weaknesses, financial details, investor names, or fundraising strategy.
